summary: Simon Späti presents a comprehensive personal knowledge management (PKM) system that combines multiple methodologies including the Second Brain, Zettelkasten, and Getting Things Done. The post argues that a well-designed PKM system reduces stress and enables a 'deeper life' by externalizing thoughts and tasks into a trusted external system. Starting from minimalism and vim's distraction-free environment, Simon built a workflow incorporating smart note-taking, linked thoughts, and systematic task management. The deeper insight is that PKM is ultimately about life itself—what we value, how we learn, and who we want to become.
key_points: Writing is not the outcome of thinking but the medium in which thought occurs—smart note-taking involves connecting thoughts rather than brainstorming from blank pages
The brain cannot distinguish between 'finished' and 'written down', so externalizing tasks and thoughts provides genuine mental relief
The PARA structure (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archive) with no more than four folder levels matches how brains naturally organize information
GTD's power comes from separating task capture from task review, and making tasks actionable rather than vague
A PKM system only works if your brain trusts it—the challenge is keeping the system updated so you don't feel compelled to remember things yourself
money_quotes: Being in a terminal with no buttons and no fancy UI, my brain focused on the essentials.
Writing is not the outcome of thinking; it is the medium in which thought occurs.
Your brain will not rest until a task is finished. As we can't complete all tasks on the spot, it helps that it does not distinguish between finished or written down.
The challenge is to keep your system up-to-date so that the brain trusts it.
Minimalism with less stuff gives you clarity and more freedom.
Instead of brainstorming, you connect thoughts and naturally generate new ideas.
Having spare money on the side is the best thing you can do for a deeper life.
Time is the currency of life, and wasting time is wasting money.
key_insight: A personal knowledge management system is not merely about organizing information—it's a framework for living deliberately, reducing cognitive load, and freeing mental space for what truly matters.
